preamble: The shift from [d] to [r] is a form of Rhotacism involving the weakening of a voiced alveolar stop into a tap or trill. This is particularly common in certain Italian dialects and in fast speech in English.

examples.note: Intervocalic [d] regularly shifts to [r] in Neapolitan.
In many English dialects, intervocalic /d/ is realized as an alveolar tap [ɾ], a rhotic sound.

sources: Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Languages: A Historical Introduction. Cambridge University Press.
Ladefoged, P. (2001). A Course in Phonetics.
